channel.title: NYT > Well
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Shoulder Pain Exercises: 7 Moves to Improve Mobility
item.description: These exercises can help you avoid discomfort by improving your strength and mobility.
item.dc:creator: Christine Yu and Theodore Tae

item.title: What Is Body Dysmorphic Disorder?
item.description: It may look like vanity, but it’s a debilitating mental health condition.
item.dc:creator: Christina Caron

item.title: How to Double Date When You Don’t Like Your Friend’s Partner
item.description: Experts share ideas on choosing the right activities and keeping the peace.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Passarella

item.title: More People Are Getting Health Advice From TikTok and Instagram. Is That a Good Thing?
item.description: Half of U.S. adults under 50 now get wellness advice from influencers. A new report examines their qualifications.
item.dc:creator: Teddy Rosenbluth

item.title: About Half of Patients with Metastatic Lung Cancer Don’t Get Treatment, Study Finds
item.description: New drugs have made America’s deadliest cancer more treatable than ever. So why aren’t they reaching patients?
item.dc:creator: Simar Bajaj

item.title: I’m Unhappily Single. Do I Have to Attend My Friend’s Wedding?
item.description: Our Ask the Therapist columnist, Lori Gottlieb, advises a reader who doesn’t want to be reminded of her loneliness yet again.
item.dc:creator: Lori Gottlieb

item.title: Should You Worry About Hantavirus?
item.description: Here’s what to know about the virus, how it spreads and the risk to the general public.
item.dc:creator: Nina Agrawal and Dani Blum

item.title: Tofersen, a New Treatment for A.L.S., Reverses Symptoms for Some
item.description: The drug is for a small subset of patients. But evidence that breathing and strength can get better for some of them is remarkable for a paralyzing, usually fatal disease.
item.dc:creator: Pam Belluck
item.media:credit: Eva Marie Uzcategui for The New York Times

item.title: Cruise Ship Linked to Hantavirus Outbreak Arrives in Spain’s Canary Islands for Disembarking
item.description: Small groups of passengers and crew left the ship after it anchored off Spain’s Canary Islands on Sunday. Those aboard were expected to be flown to their home countries, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Carlos Barragán, Amelia Nierenberg and Lynsey Chutel
item.media:description: A passenger from the MV Hondius speaking with medical staff in the Granadilla Port in Tenerife, Canary Islands, on Sunday.

item.title: Passengers on Hantavirus Cruise Ship Will Disembark in Tenerife and Return to Home Countries
item.description: The cruise ship will arrive on the island of Tenerife, part of the Canary Islands of Spain, officials said. All of the passengers will then be evacuated to their home countries.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald and Carlos Barragán
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Workers on the island of Tenerife setting up tents at an expected reception point for passengers from the cruise ship MV Hondius.

item.title: Hantavirus Vaccines and Treatments Are in the Pipeline
item.description: But it has been hard to attract interest in medical interventions for viruses that have not been considered a top public health priority, scientists say.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.media:description: An ambulance boat approaching the cruise ship MV Hondius, which has sustained a deadly hantavirus outbreak, off Cape Verde on Tuesday.

item.title: Norovirus Outbreak Sickens 115 on Cruise Ship
item.description: More than 100 passengers and 13 crew members on the Caribbean Princess reported being ill, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: The Caribbean Princess left Port Everglades in Fort Lauderdale, Fla., on April 28 and is expected to return to Florida on Monday.

item.title: Experts Stress Hantavirus Risk Remains Low As Officials Track Cruise Ship Passengers
item.description: Health officials are closely monitoring those who were exposed for symptoms of the virus, which does not spread easily between humans.
item.dc:creator: Dani Blum

item.title: Hantavirus Is Nothing Like Coronavirus, but It’s Bringing Some ‘Covid P.T.S.D.’
item.description: Experts have been quick to reassure the public after the deaths aboard the Dutch cruise ship MV Hondius, but images and turns of phrase have rekindled anxieties from Covid’s early days.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:description: Health workers in protective gear evacuating patients from the MV Hondius cruise ship yesterday.

item.title: Employees With Medical Conditions Challenge C.D.C. In-Office Requirement
item.description: The health agency has been particularly strict in abolishing at-home work, overriding accommodations that were granted years before the pandemic.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Katie Schenk, an infectious disease epidemiologist, was hired as a remote C.D.C. employee in 2024 and lost her telework accommodation. After trying in vain to get it back she quit the agency in frustration in February.

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Some Delight to Your Day
item.description: Buried treasure, birds on the move and secret library access.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Tiny Love Stories: ‘I Could Feel Him Watching Me’
item.description: Modern Love in miniature, featuring reader-submitted stories of no more than 100 words.

item.title: American Factories Lag in Adopting A.I. This Drugmaker Is an Exception.
item.description: A Bristol Myers Squibb plant that makes cancer drugs was the only manufacturer in the U.S. recognized for innovation by the World Economic Forum this year.
item.dc:creator: Farah Stockman
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: At the Bristol Myers Squibb facility in Devens, Mass., scientists use automated machines to help make cancer drugs.

item.title: TikTok Has Discovered Tae Bo, the Beloved ’90s Workout
item.description: A younger generation is embracing the nostalgic fitness craze.
item.dc:creator: Chloe W. Shakin

item.title: Hantavirus Outbreaks Are Rare, but They Aren’t Going Away and There’s No Cure
item.description: Since the family of rodent-borne infections were identified in the 1950s, they have turned up all over the world.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:description: A cruise ship remained stranded off the coast of Cape Verde after a suspected hantavirus outbreak on the vessel killed at least three people and left several others ill.

item.title: Inside Meatstock, the Carnivore Diet Convention Where Steak Is Medicine
item.description: Devotees of the diet, which Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy, Jr. follows, bonded over brisket and butter at Meatstock.
item.dc:creator: Dani Blum and Juan Diego Reyes for The New York Times
item.media:credit: Juan Diego Reyes for The New York Times

item.title: Is Self-Tanner Bad for You?
item.description: Sunless tanning products are billed as safer than tanning in the sun. We asked experts if they come with any health risks of their own.
item.dc:creator: Melinda Wenner Moyer
item.media:credit: Eric Helgas for The New York Times

item.title: How a Hantavirus Infection Feels: ‘You Just Can’t Get the Air In’
item.description: Doctors say that the virus, which is rare, can quickly progress from flulike symptoms to something far more serious.
item.dc:creator: Simar Bajaj and Nina Agrawal

item.title: Mifepristone, the Abortion Pill, Returns to the Supreme Court. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: The Louisiana lawsuit, which seeks to block telehealth prescribing and mailing of the abortion pill mifepristone, has major implications for abortion access nationwide.
item.dc:creator: Pam Belluck
item.media:credit: Hannah Yoon for The New York Times
item.media:description: After an appeals court ruling Friday, some telehealth abortion services started shipping only misoprostol, typically the second drug used after mifepristone in the medication abortion regimen. Services resumed sending both medications on Monday after the Supreme Court temporarily restored access.

item.title: A Cavity Doesn’t Always Need a Filling
item.description: How to weigh your dental options.
item.dc:creator: Erik Vance

item.title: How Your Gut Can Affect Your Brain
item.description: Scientists keep learning more about the connection between the brain and the gastrointestinal system.
item.dc:creator: Christina Caron

item.title: What to Know About the Hantavirus Outbreak on an Atlantic Cruise Ship
item.description: Health authorities said three passengers from the MV Hondius had died after showing symptoms of the rare disease.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses and Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: The cruise ship MV Hondius stationed off the port of Praia, Cape Verde, on Sunday.

item.title: What Doctors Want You to Know About Cannabis and Health
item.description: The government recently loosened medical marijuana rules. Experts separate fact from fiction about the drug’s safety and benefits.
item.dc:creator: Roni Caryn Rabin

item.title: What to Know About Orphines, a New Class of Deadly Opioids
item.description: The drugs are 10 times more dangerous than fentanyl. They are showing up in street drugs in the South and the Midwest, and will most likely spread to other regions.
item.dc:creator: Jan Hoffman
item.media:credit: Brett Carlsen for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Knoxville, Tenn., police officer at the scene of a suspected overdose last month. The greater Knoxville area has seen a startling spike in overdose deaths driven by the presence of orphines.

item.title: A Medical Examiner Chases Down an Elusive Killer
item.description: Unusual opioids are infiltrating street drugs. Knoxville’s top forensic doctor is on the front lines, pressing to quickly identify the most lethal.
item.dc:creator: Jan Hoffman and Brett Carlsen
item.media:credit: Brett Carlsen for The New York Times
